# Last Time

::: {.incremental}
+ **Reference Variables**
+ **Pass by Value and Pass by Reference**
+ **Reference Variables as Parameters**
+ **Static Local Variables**
+ **Default Parameters**
+ **Exit**
+ **Menus and Functions**
:::

::: notes
Brief recall of lecture 05.01. Students should remember that parameter style controls how data crosses a function boundary, and reference parameters can make caller-owned data change.
:::

<!-- Slide 1 -->

# Today's Agenda

- Function Overloading
- Choosing a Parameter Style
- Function Side Effects

::: notes

:::

<!-- Slide 2 -->

{{< include topic_1_function_overloading.qmd >}}
{{< include topic_2_parameter_style.qmd >}}
{{< include topic_3_function_side_effects.qmd >}}

---

# Key Takeaways

- Function overloading lets related functions share one name when their parameter lists are different.
- A function signature is the function name plus the parameter types and count.
- Parameter style is part of the function contract.
- Side effects are changes outside a returned value; good function design makes them intentional and traceable.

<!-- Slide 47 -->

---

# Next Time

<!-- Slide 48 -->

---

# Appendix

<!-- Slide 49 -->

---

## Function Overloading: Good Practices

- Overload functions when the operation is conceptually the same.
- Keep the overload set small enough that each version is easy to predict.
- Prefer clear parameter lists over clever combinations.

<!-- Slide 50 -->

---

## Function Overloading: Common Mistakes

- Trying to overload by return type alone.
- Creating overloads whose calls are ambiguous.
- Reusing the same name for functions that do different jobs.

<!-- Slide 51 -->

---

## Function Overloading: More Examples

```cpp
void print(int value);
void print(double value);
void print(string value);
```

All three functions share the same name, but the parameter type changes.

<!-- Slide 52 -->

---

## Function Overloading: Practice Problems

1. Write two `total` prototypes with different parameter counts.
2. Decide whether `double value()` and `int value()` are valid overloads.
3. Predict which overload is called by `show(4.0)`.

<!-- Slide 53 -->

---

## Function Overloading: AI Search Terms

- "C++ function overloading beginner examples"
- "C++ function signature overload resolution"
- "C++ overload return type not enough"

<!-- Slide 54 -->

---

## Function Overloading: Questions for Reflection

- When does one shared function name make code easier to read?
- Why does the compiler need the parameter list to choose an overload?

<!-- Slide 55 -->

---

## Choosing a Parameter Style: Good Practices

- Use return values for one computed answer.
- Use non-const references only when changing caller data is part of the contract.
- Use `const` reference for read-only large data.

<!-- Slide 56 -->

---

## Choosing a Parameter Style: Common Mistakes

- Passing by reference when the function only needs to read.
- Returning one value while also changing unrelated caller variables.
- Hiding important data movement inside the function body.

<!-- Slide 57 -->

---

## Choosing a Parameter Style: More Examples

```cpp
double average(const vector<int>& scores);
void resetScore(int& score);
double adjustedScore(double score);
```

The prototype tells whether data is read, changed, or returned.

<!-- Slide 58 -->

---

## Choosing a Parameter Style: Practice Problems

1. Choose a parameter style for a function that displays a vector.
2. Choose a style for a function that resets a balance to zero.
3. Choose a style for a function that computes a sales tax total.

<!-- Slide 59 -->

---

## Choosing a Parameter Style: AI Search Terms

- "C++ pass by value vs reference"
- "C++ const reference parameter"
- "C++ function parameter design"

<!-- Slide 60 -->

---

## Choosing a Parameter Style: Questions for Reflection

- How can a prototype warn you that caller data may change?
- Why is returning a new value often easier to trace than modifying by reference?

<!-- Slide 61 -->

---

## Function Side Effects: Good Practices

- Keep calculation functions return-based when practical.
- Make intended mutation visible through the parameter list.
- Avoid hidden state unless the function clearly owns that state.

<!-- Slide 62 -->

---

## Function Side Effects: Common Mistakes

- Changing caller data unexpectedly.
- Depending on global data when parameters would be clearer.
- Writing functions that calculate, display, and mutate at the same time.

<!-- Slide 63 -->

---

## Function Side Effects: More Examples

```cpp
double addTax(double price);
void addTax(double& price);
void displayTotal(double total);
```

These functions have different effects even when their names look related.

<!-- Slide 64 -->

---

## Function Side Effects: Practice Problems

1. Identify the side effect in a function that prints to `cout`.
2. Identify the side effect in a function with an `int&` parameter.
3. Rewrite a mutation function so it returns a new value instead.

<!-- Slide 65 -->

---

## Function Side Effects: AI Search Terms

- "C++ function side effects"
- "pure function vs side effect C++"
- "C++ reference parameter mutation"

<!-- Slide 66 -->

---

## Function Side Effects: Questions for Reflection

- Which side effects are useful in a menu program?
- Which side effects make a function harder to test?

<!-- Slide 67 -->
